mnemonics.title: DIC - Divide, Integrate, Combine
mnemonics.content: Divide the problem, Integrate sub-solutions recursively, Combine results. This is the core D&C paradigm for both Merge and Quick Sort.

mnemonics.title: MRS. DIM
mnemonics.content: Merge Recursively Sort - Divide Into Middle. Remember: Split array at middle, recursively sort halves, merge sorted halves.

mnemonics.title: Quick! Pick A Pivot, Partition, Then Sort
mnemonics.content: Q-PAPS: Quick Sort works by selecting a pivot, partitioning elements around it (smaller left, larger right), then recursively sorting partitions.

mnemonics.title: M-Quick-Remember
mnemonics.content: Merge Sort: Stable, O(n log n) guaranteed. Quick Sort: Not stable, O(n log n) average but O(n²) worst case. Remember: 'M' for 'More Predictable', 'Q' for 'Quick but Unpredictable'.

acronyms.usage: The algorithmic paradigm where a problem is divided into smaller subproblems, solved recursively, and solutions combined.
In Quick Sort, the chosen element used to partition the array; all smaller elements go left, larger go right.

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity recurrence for Merge Sort
acronyms.usage: Solving this recurrence gives O(n log n) - the time complexity of Merge Sort.

acronyms.expansion: Internal Navigation Performed Locally Inside Current Environment
acronyms.usage: Quick Sort can be in-place (O(1) extra space), while Merge Sort requires O(n) extra space for merging.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a binary tree being built downward. Start with one array at top, keep splitting into two halves until single elements at bottom leaves. This is the 'Divide' phase.
Picture two sorted stacks of cards being merged like a shuffle - always pick the smaller top card from either stack. Build merged sorted array from bottom up.
Visualize a pivot as a person standing in a line. Everyone smaller moves to their left, everyone larger moves to their right. The pivot finds its final position.

Merge Sort: Always forms complete binary tree (log n levels), each level processes n elements = n log n. Quick Sort: Good pivot gives balanced tree; bad pivot (already sorted) gives skewed tree = O(n²).

analogies.analog: Think of two sorted piles of exam papers (by roll number). To combine them into one sorted pile, you compare the top paper of each pile and take the smaller one - repeatedly until done.
Imagine organizing books on a shelf by height. Pick a book as reference (pivot), then arrange all shorter books to its left and taller books to its right - like partitioning by height.
Like solving a jigsaw puzzle: divide into corners, edges, and middle sections, solve each section separately, then combine all parts into the final picture.
Stable (Merge Sort): If two students have same marks, their original order in class list is preserved. Unstable (Quick Sort): Same marks students may swap positions - original order not guaranteed.
